competitors.label: MDN JavaScript execution model
competitors.selectionReason: This is MDN's canonical explanation of the event loop, call stack, and job queues, so it is the strongest docs baseline for this topic.
theirStrengths: It gives the authoritative runtime model and names the queue/job concepts precisely.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly framed around frontend paint starvation and UI-debug scenarios than our trivia.

realWorldUseCases: That under-the-hood order is the real production pitfall: many developers assume any async boundary yields rendering time, but a long microtask chain can still freeze the screen.
actionableExamples: Spinner may still not paint before the microtasks finish.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the runtime model precisely, but it is less explicit about a real paint-starvation debugging case.
actionableExamples: Its examples explain queues well, while our page now includes a concrete spinner-plus-microtasks failure and a paint-first fix.
followUpConfusion: MDN still has broader execution-model depth, so the larger follow-up comparison remains close.
Add one short note on scheduler APIs or requestIdleCallback so the advanced scheduling follow-up set is broader.

competitors.winReason: MDN remains the canonical execution-model reference, but our page is now stronger on the specific frontend bug pattern of paint starvation and on how to fix it.

competitors.label: javascript.info Event loop
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is one of the clearest tutorial references for queue ordering and rendering behavior, so it is a fair tutorial comparator.
theirStrengths: It explains queue order, long tasks, and paint timing with concrete runnable examples.
theirGaps: It is broader on scheduling mechanics than on the specific production-debug framing we use.

realWorldUseCases: That under-the-hood order is the real production pitfall: many developers assume any async boundary yields rendering time, but a long microtask chain can still freeze the screen.
actionableExamples: The follow-up rule is simple: await Promise.resolve() still stays in microtask territory, so it does not guarantee a paint.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info already ties the event loop to responsiveness, but our page now frames the issue through a paint-starved spinner bug.
actionableExamples: Its worked examples are still strong, while our page now adds a direct before-fix and after-fix pattern for UI rendering.
followUpConfusion: It still answers scheduling and rendering follow-ups well enough that the broader comparison stays close.
Add one tiny timeline diagram that shows click, microtasks, paint, and timer order in a single view.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info remains a great queue-order tutorial, but our page is now more specific about UI paint starvation and the concrete fix pattern frontend developers need.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Event loop
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep answer for the same event-loop intent, so it is the strongest interview calibration point.
competitors.winReason: Our page now goes further on paint starvation, microtask-heavy UI bugs, and the requestAnimationFrame/setTimeout fix pattern, while GreatFrontEnd stays closer to interview-definition level.
theirStrengths: It is tightly aligned with the interview phrasing and cleanly separates call stack, task queue, and loop responsibilities.
theirGaps: It is lighter on concrete UI-freeze and paint-timing pitfalls than our current trivia.

realWorldUseCases: That under-the-hood order is the real production pitfall: many developers assume any async boundary yields rendering time, but a long microtask chain can still freeze the screen.
GreatFrontEnd explains the loop well, but it is lighter on a concrete paint-starvation bug pattern.
actionableExamples: Its interview walkthrough is concise, while our page now includes both the failure pattern and the paint-first fix.
followUpConfusion: Its call-stack versus task-queue explanation remains competitive, so the broader follow-up comparison stays close.
